research_question: Épidémiologie des scapulalgies et pathologies de la coiffe des rotateurs chez l'adulte

claims.text: The incidence of rotator cuff tears increases with aging, with more than half of individuals in their 80s having a rotator cuff tear.
Smoking, hypercholesterolemia, and genetics are identified as risk factors for rotator cuff disease.
Rotator cuff disease etiology is multifactorial, including age-related degeneration and micro/macrotrauma.
Rotator cuff abnormalities become more common with age: prevalence rises from 9.7% in those under 20 to 62% in those 80 and older (OR 15, 95% CI 9.6-24).
Many rotator cuff abnormalities are asymptomatic, and the presence of an abnormality does not necessarily correlate with symptoms.
Shoulder pain is among the most common regional musculoskeletal complaints in the work environment.
Only a small proportion of shoulder pain at work can be explained by conditions at the workplace.
Upper-limb work-related musculoskeletal disorders are among the most common work-related diseases.
Absence of accessible diagnostic criteria and etiological cause-effect demonstration are barriers to understanding shoulder WRMSDs.
Rotator cuff disease is among the most common musculoskeletal disorders with high direct and indirect costs in industrialized countries.
Not all rotator cuff tears are symptomatic.
Genetic factors have been investigated as involved in the pathogenesis of rotator cuff pathology.
Shoulder pain is one of the most frequent musculoskeletal complaints, with prevalence and consequences increasing with age.
Annual incidence of shoulder pain was 2.4% in adults aged 45-64 (17.3/1000 person-years), 12.8/1000 in 65-74, and 6.7/1000 in 75+.
Rotator cuff imaging abnormalities (tendinopathy, tears, calcification) are prevalent in asymptomatic adult shoulders.
Population-based incidence of acute full-thickness rotator cuff tears was estimated using a diagnostic model.
In 362 patients, arthroscopic rotator cuff repair was 29.2% more expensive than open surgery; each additional hospital day increased costs by 18.9%; clinical improvement was significant in 84.8%.
Point and lifetime prevalence of shoulder pain were assessed in 500 middle-aged women aged 45-65 years.
Risk factors for shoulder pain in middle-aged women were explored.
Rotator cuff tears are a common cause of pain and disability of the shoulder.
The contralateral shoulder is at risk of rotator cuff tears in patients with symptomatic tears.
The societal economic burden of shoulder pain was calculated for patients on orthopedic waiting lists at an Australian public hospital.
Both government healthcare costs and societal costs (including lost productivity) were assessed.
Patients with subacromial impingement syndrome lost an average of 27 workdays per six months (95% CI: 18-40), three times more than the 9 days previously reported for shoulder problems in general.
Subacromial impingement syndrome is the most common shoulder disorder.
High incidence of lost workdays was observed in patients with subacromial impingement syndrome.
Shoulder work-related musculoskeletal disorders were assessed across 15 industries in China.
Changes in modern industrial production practices are associated with increased shoulder WRMSDs prevalence across sectors.
Prevalence of asymptomatic rotator cuff tears was evaluated in a Korean population sample of 486 volunteers (70.4% female).
Risk factors related to asymptomatic rotator cuff tears were identified.

Among 102,488 US patients, post-index costs were $32,110 for full-thickness rotator cuff tears; absenteeism-related productivity losses were $5,843; delayed surgery added $8,524 per partial-thickness case.
Delayed surgical intervention has economic implications for rotator cuff tears.
Risk factors for surgery due to rotator cuff disease were evaluated in a large population-based cohort.
Few risk factors for rotator cuff disease have been firmly established.
Prevalence and characteristics of musculoskeletal complaints in primary care were analyzed using electronic health record data.
Calcific tendinitis of the shoulder has compensation consequences as an occupational disease in France.
Risk factors for rotator cuff injury among dentists were identified in a hospital environment.
Study aimed to improve preventive measures for professional risks in a hospital environment.
A significant proportion of the population worldwide experiences shoulder pain daily, yearly, and throughout a lifetime.
Regional gaps in evidence and methodological inconsistencies exist in shoulder pain epidemiology.
The annual consultation prevalence for shoulder pain conditions is approximately 1%, similar in women and men.
About two thirds of patients with shoulder pain consulted a doctor only once.
Impingement and rotator cuff syndromes are the most frequent shoulder pain diagnoses.
Age was the strongest predictor for incident cases of rotator cuff syndrome in a large French working population.
Arm abduction was the major work-related risk factor for rotator cuff syndrome for both genders.
Lack of social support was a predictor for rotator cuff syndrome among men.
Age was the strongest predictor of incident shoulder pain in both genders in a large French working population.
BMI and biomechanical and psychosocial factors were identified as risk factors for shoulder pain.
No factor related to work organization remained in final predictive models.
Narrative review synthesizing the epidemiology of shoulder pain from published literature.
Personal, biomechanical, and psychosocial risk factors for rotator cuff syndrome were examined in 3,710 workers representative of a French region's working population.
Workers were exposed to various levels of shoulder constraints in a French regional population.
Cumulative occupational shoulder exposures carried an increased risk of surgery for subacromial impingement syndrome, with similar exposure-response curves for men and women.
Risk of surgery for SIS increased gradually when the period of exposure assessment was extended.
In the general working population, a substantial fraction of first-time SIS surgeries could be related to cumulative occupational shoulder exposures.
Limited evidence exists for an association between arm elevation at work and shoulder disorders.
Severe arm elevation with elbows above shoulder level (>90°) shows moderate evidence for association with shoulder disorders.
Rotator cuff-related shoulder pain is a common upper limb complaint potentially associated with cardiometabolic risk factors.
Evidence was synthesized on the association between metabolic syndrome and rotator cuff-related shoulder pain.
Rotator cuff surgery rates are increasing in Chile, concordant with reports from other western countries.
Patients' health insurance type was the most important factor associated with rotator cuff surgery rate, with higher rates in the private sector.
Medical service use, frequency and cost distributions for major shoulder disorders in Korea were assessed using nationwide claims data.
Occupational upper-extremity load and posture exposures were associated with increased risk for surgical treatment of subacromial impingement syndrome.
The study underlines the need for reducing workplace exposures and early symptom detection in highly exposed occupational groups.
